Dimml.history: Dimml appears to be a small SaaS tool. Minimal public information is available about its founding, ownership, or product details.
Dimml.summary_abuse: Dimml is a niche platform with no documented abuse incidents. Insufficient public information is available to assess its characteristics in detail.

dimple.history: dimple.js was an open-source d3-based charting library created around 2013 for building bar, line, area, and scatter charts. The project is no longer actively maintained.
dimple.summary_abuse: dimple.js is a charting library with no notable abuse signal. It is a pure frontend data visualization tool.

Dina Kunder.history: Dina Kunder is a Norwegian CRM and loyalty platform for small businesses, helping SMBs manage customer relationships and loyalty programs. The company operates primarily in the Norwegian market.
Dina Kunder.summary_abuse: Dina Kunder is a niche Norwegian CRM and loyalty platform with no documented abuse incidents. It serves small businesses in the local Norwegian market.

DirectAdmin.history: DirectAdmin was developed by JBMC Software in 2003 as a web-based hosting control panel aimed at simplifying server administration. Headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, the software competes with cPanel and Plesk. It remained independently owned by JBMC Software throughout its history with no major acquisitions or rebrands.
DirectAdmin.summary_abuse: DirectAdmin is a server-side hosting control panel with limited direct abuse exposure. Attackers sometimes target poorly secured DirectAdmin installations as part of broader server compromise campaigns. The software itself does not facilitate user-generated content hosting or anonymous signups; abuse vectors are primarily unauthorized access to misconfigured servers. The vendor provides security advisories and regular updates.

Directorist.history: Directorist is a WordPress directory plugin developed by WPWax, a Bangladeshi company founded by Masud Rana. The plugin provides AI-powered business directory and classified ad functionality for WordPress sites. It has grown to over 20,000 active installs and is maintained actively on WordPress.org.
Directorist.summary_abuse: Directorist is a WordPress directory plugin with low direct abuse risk. As with all directory-building tools, sites built with it could theoretically be used to list fake or fraudulent businesses, but abuse is rare and the plugin itself has no mechanism for facilitating such listings beyond standard WordPress functionality. The vendor does not have a notable abuse history.

Directus.history: Directus was created in 2012 as an open-source data management platform built on top of SQL databases. Initially developed as a simple database GUI, it evolved into a full headless CMS. In 2018, Directus 7 introduced a complete rewrite in Vue.js. The project relicensed from GPL to Business Source License (BSL 1.1) in 2020 and raised venture funding. It has strong GitHub presence with over 25,000 stars.
Directus.summary_abuse: Directus is a headless CMS and data platform with minimal abuse exposure. It is primarily self-hosted enterprise infrastructure used by development teams. No notable abuse patterns have been documented, and the vendor maintains an active security disclosure policy. The BSL license restricts commercial competitors from repackaging the software.

Direktonline.history: Direktonline is a Swedish web agency founded in 2006 and headquartered in Gothenburg, providing website design, e-commerce, SEO, and copywriting services. The company has a team of 2-10 employees and has completed over 1,500 projects. No notable ownership changes or controversies on record.
Direktonline.summary_abuse: Direktonline is a small Swedish web agency with no notable abuse history. As a professional services company rather than a platform, it presents minimal direct abuse risk. No documented cases of misuse or fraudulent activity associated with the company.

Disciple.history: Disciple was founded in 2013 by Benji Vaughan and Jeremy Smith in London, UK. Initially focused on building fan engagement apps for musicians as an agency model, it pivoted to a global SaaS platform for community building. In 2021, the company raised $6 million in Series A funding. As of 2026, it serves over 700 communities with 2 million members.
Disciple.summary_abuse: Disciple is a community-building SaaS platform with low abuse exposure. As a closed, moderated community environment, it does not host public user-generated content visible to non-members. Abuse risk is limited to potential policy violations within hosted communities. The vendor enforces terms of service and content moderation guidelines.

Discourse.history: Discourse was founded in 2013 by Jeff Atwood (co-founder of Stack Overflow), Robin Ward, and Sam Saffron. It was built as a modern open-source forum platform to replace aging forum software like phpBB and vBulletin. The company behind it, Civilized Discourse Construction Kit, Inc., raised VC funding and offers both self-hosted and hosted versions. Discourse has become widely adopted by developer communities, open-source projects, and enterprises.
Discourse.summary_abuse: Discourse is a forum platform that can be self-hosted or used as a managed service. Forums built on Discourse can attract spam and abusive content, but the platform includes robust moderation tools including rate limiting, spam detection (via Akismet), and admin controls. The Discourse team actively maintains security patches. Self-hosted instances vary in moderation quality depending on the operator.

Discuz! X.history: Discuz! was created by Dai Zhikang in March 2002 as Crossday Bulletin (CDB) and renamed Discuz! with its October 2002 release. In February 2003, Comsenz Technology Co., Ltd. was incorporated to develop and commercialize the software. Discuz! X 1.0 was released in 2010, adding SNS and open platform features. Tencent acquired Comsenz in August 2010, making Discuz! a Tencent subsidiary product. It became the dominant forum software in China.
Discuz! X.summary_abuse: Discuz! X is a Chinese forum platform primarily used in China under Tencent ownership. It powers a large number of Chinese forums and is subject to Chinese content regulations including censorship requirements. Security vulnerabilities in older Discuz! versions have been exploited in the wild to compromise forum installations. It does not align with Western GDPR standards. Abuse patterns are primarily related to spam forums and SEO spam installations on unpatched versions.

DISH.history: DISH Digital Solutions GmbH was founded in 2015 as a wholly owned subsidiary of METRO AG (the German wholesale and food service giant), initially called Hospitality Digital. In 2018, METRO rebranded the platform as DISH and expanded it as a digital community for independent restaurateurs.
DISH.summary_abuse: DISH is a restaurant management and ordering platform operated by METRO AG with no notable abuse signal. It serves independent restaurateurs with reservation and ordering tools and is backed by a large European publicly-traded company.

Disqus.history: Disqus was founded in 2007 by Daniel Ha and Jason Yan while at Y Combinator. It grew into the dominant web commenting platform embedded on millions of sites. In December 2017, Disqus was acquired by Zeta Global for approximately $90 million. In 2018, it emerged that Disqus had been quietly inserting crypto-mining scripts; the practice was halted after public backlash. The free tier embeds advertising and collects user data for Zeta Global's marketing platform.
Disqus.summary_abuse: Disqus is a widely embedded commenting platform with a notable privacy footprint. The free tier uses advertising and tracks users across sites for Zeta Global's ad-tech platform, resulting in heavy tracker density. In 2017, Disqus was found to have been running undisclosed cryptocurrency mining scripts on embedded pages; the behavior was stopped following public exposure. Comment sections on Disqus can attract spam and harassing content, and the platform has faced criticism for slow removal of abusive content. GDPR compliance has been questioned due to the tracking model.

Distributor.history: Distributor is an open-source WordPress content syndication plugin developed and released by 10up in 2017. It enables content sharing between WordPress sites within a multisite network or across separate WordPress installations. 10up is a well-regarded WordPress development agency and the plugin has been actively maintained since its release.
Distributor.summary_abuse: Distributor is a server-side WordPress plugin for syndicating content between sites. It has no notable abuse exposure as it requires authentication and admin access to configure. The plugin does not expose any public-facing APIs that could be exploited without credentials. No documented abuse incidents.

District M.history: District M was founded in 2013 in Montreal, Canada by Jean-Francois Cote, Patrice Marin, Sebastien Filion, and others as a programmatic advertising exchange and ad network. The company raised approximately $39 million in total funding including a $12 million round in 2019. In February 2021, district m merged with Sharethrough, Inc., a native advertising platform, combining to form a larger independent SSP.
District M.summary_abuse: District M is a programmatic advertising platform that operates as an ad exchange and supply-side platform. As an ad-tech intermediary, it is associated with heavy tracker density and cross-site user tracking inherent to programmatic advertising. Ad exchanges can inadvertently serve malvertising if demand-side partners are not properly vetted. The company follows IAB standards and industry practices for transparency.

Dito.history: DITO Telecommunity was established from Mindanao Islamic Telephone Company (Mislatel), which received Congressional franchise via Republic Act No. 8627 in April 1998. In July 2019, Mislatel was renamed DITO Telecommunity after a consortium involving Udenna Corporation and China Telecom won the third telco franchise in the Philippines. DITO officially launched commercial mobile services in March 2021, becoming the Philippines' third major mobile network operator.
Dito.summary_abuse: DITO is a Philippine telecommunications company that is the country's third mobile network operator. As a telco, it is subject to Philippine regulatory oversight. Concerns have been raised about its tie to China Telecom, a state-owned enterprise, particularly around data sovereignty. No major documented abuse incidents specific to fraudulent use of the DITO platform have been reported.

Ditto.history: Ditto (ditto.live) was founded around 2018 and is headquartered in the United States. The company provides an edge-native mobile database SDK that enables peer-to-peer data synchronization using Bluetooth, WiFi, and other transports without requiring a persistent internet connection. Ditto uses CRDT (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types) technology for conflict resolution and targets use cases in aviation, retail, and military environments.
Ditto.summary_abuse: Ditto is a backend database SDK for edge and IoT applications. It has no consumer-facing surface and is embedded within mobile and IoT applications. No notable abuse patterns are documented. The SDK is used in enterprise applications requiring offline-first data synchronization.

Divhunt.history: Divhunt was co-founded in 2019 in Belgrade, Serbia. The platform started as a niche tool originally tailored for local travel agencies and evolved into a low-code and no-code visual website builder for designers and developers. The company is a small independent startup competing with Webflow and other visual builders.
Divhunt.summary_abuse: Divhunt is a small no-code website builder with minimal documented abuse exposure. As a website hosting platform, it could theoretically host fraudulent pages, but the platform is niche and has no documented history of being targeted by bad actors. The vendor enforces terms of service.

Divi.history: Divi is a WordPress page builder and theme created by Elegant Themes, founded by Nick Roach. Divi was first released in December 2013 as one of the first visual drag-and-drop WordPress page builders. Elegant Themes was founded circa 2008 and is based in the United States. Divi has grown to become one of the most widely used WordPress page builders with millions of active installs.
Divi.summary_abuse: Divi is a self-hosted WordPress page builder theme with low direct abuse exposure. Like all WordPress-based products, sites built with Divi can be compromised if running outdated versions, but the plugin itself is not disproportionately abused. Occasionally, nulled (pirated) versions of Divi circulate containing malicious code injections. The vendor actively releases security updates.

Divide.history: Divide is a technology company with limited publicly available information. Based on available records, it appears to be a niche SaaS product with minimal public presence. No notable ownership changes or significant events are documented.
Divide.summary_abuse: Divide has no notable documented abuse history. The platform's limited public presence means there is minimal information about its abuse posture or takedown responsiveness.

DivideBuy.history: DivideBuy was founded in the UK around 2014 as a buy now pay later (BNPL) platform offering interest-free installment credit at point of sale for UK retailers. In September 2021, the company secured £300 million in investment. In February 2023, DivideBuy was acquired by Zopa, the UK digital bank, to power its retail finance solution.
DivideBuy.summary_abuse: DivideBuy is a UK buy now pay later platform now operating under Zopa's banking license. As a regulated credit provider, it is subject to FCA oversight. BNPL products carry general consumer credit risk including debt accumulation concerns, but DivideBuy operates within the UK regulatory framework. No specific fraud or abuse incidents specific to the DivideBuy platform have been widely documented.

Divido.history: Divido was founded in 2014 in London by Christer Holloman, Fredrik Lindstrom, and Anders Hallberg as a white-label BNPL and retail finance platform. The company raised $45 million in total, including a $30 million Series B in June 2021 backed by HSBC, ING, and American Express Ventures. In July 2024, Divido entered administration and filed for bankruptcy, having never turned a profit despite operating in ten markets.
Divido.summary_abuse: Divido was a white-label BNPL platform that entered administration in 2024. As a B2B lending infrastructure provider, it had limited direct consumer abuse exposure. The company's collapse left merchant partners without service. No specific fraud incidents involving Divido were widely documented prior to its insolvency.

Dixa.history: Dixa was founded in 2015 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Mads Fosselius, Jakob Nederby Nielsen, Krisztian Tabori, and Andrew Rolfe as a customer service and CX platform. The company raised $158 million in Series C funding from General Atlantic in 2021. In 2022, Dixa acquired Miuros and Solvemate to expand its AI and chatbot capabilities. Dixa competes with Zendesk and Intercom in the customer service software market.
Dixa.summary_abuse: Dixa is a B2B customer service SaaS platform used by support teams. It has minimal abuse exposure as access requires business account setup and payment. No documented cases of abuse or fraud associated with the Dixa platform.

Django.history: Django was created in 2003 by Adrian Holovaty and Simon Willison at the Lawrence Journal-World newspaper in Lawrence, Kansas. It was open-sourced in July 2005. The Django Software Foundation (DSF) was formed in 2008 to support the project. Django pioneered the rapid web development paradigm with its ORM, admin interface, and convention-over-configuration approach. It remains one of the most widely used Python web frameworks, powering sites like Instagram, Disqus, and Pinterest.
Django.summary_abuse: Django is a foundational Python web framework with no direct abuse surface. Like all server-side frameworks, web applications built with Django can have vulnerabilities, but the framework itself includes robust built-in protections against SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and clickjacking. The Django Security team actively publishes CVE advisories and security releases. No abuse concerns are attributable to the framework itself.

Django CMS.history: Django CMS was launched by Divio AG in 2007 as an open-source CMS built on the Django framework. The django CMS Association was formed in 2020 to take over governance of the project as an independent, community-driven organization separate from Divio. The project is BSD-licensed and hosted on GitHub with an active contributor community.
Django CMS.summary_abuse: Django CMS is a self-hosted open-source content management system with no direct abuse surface. Sites built with Django CMS are subject to the same general web application threats as any Django site, but the CMS itself does not facilitate abuse. The project maintains security disclosures through GitHub.

DM Polopoly.history: Polopoly was founded in 2000 in Stockholm, Sweden as a Java-based web CMS, becoming Scandinavia's largest CMS vendor. In 2008, media software company Atex acquired Polopoly for $23.3 million. Atex itself was later acquired by Aquila (a Constellation Software subsidiary) in 2017. The platform is now branded as DM Polopoly under Atex.
DM Polopoly.summary_abuse: DM Polopoly is an enterprise CMS used by media and publishing organizations with no notable abuse signal. It is a licensed enterprise software product without a consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

DNN.history: DotNetNuke (DNN) originated from Microsoft's IBuySpy Portal reference application in 2001. Shaun Walker released a significantly enhanced version as IBuySpy Workshop on December 24, 2002, then rebranded it DotNetNuke in March 2003. By 2009, it was the leading .NET open-source CMS. In 2013, the company was renamed DNN Corporation and commercial editions were rebranded as Evoq. In 2017, ESW Capital acquired DNN Corp. and transferred stewardship of the open-source project to the .NET Foundation. The platform has been downloaded over 8 million times and powers over 750,000 websites.
DNN.summary_abuse: DNN (DotNetNuke) is a self-hosted ASP.NET CMS platform used by a wide range of organizations. Like most CMS platforms, it can be deployed by malicious actors to host phishing pages or scam sites, particularly older or unpatched installations. Several CVEs have been disclosed for DNN over the years. However, legitimate enterprise and government use dominates, and the .NET Foundation manages responsible disclosure.

DocFX.history: DocFX is an open-source static documentation site generator for .NET projects, initially released by Microsoft around 2016 under the MIT license. It was used by Microsoft internally for documentation generation. In November 2022, Microsoft announced it would no longer use DocFX for Microsoft Learn and does not intend to continue supporting the project as a primary sponsor. The project continues as a community-driven initiative under the .NET Foundation.
DocFX.summary_abuse: DocFX is a documentation generation tool for .NET projects. It generates static websites from source code and markdown and has no consumer-facing surface or hosting component. No abuse concerns are documented.

Docker.history: Docker was started as dotCloud by Solomon Hykes in 2008 in Paris, France, originally a platform-as-a-service company. The Docker containerization technology was open-sourced in March 2013 and the company was renamed Docker Inc. It rapidly became the industry standard for containerization. In November 2019, Docker sold its enterprise division to Mirantis to refocus on developer tools. Docker Desktop licensing was changed to a paid subscription for large companies in 2022, sparking debate in the developer community.
Docker.summary_abuse: Docker Hub, the public container registry, has historically hosted malicious images including cryptomining malware and credential stealers disguised as legitimate software. Docker has implemented image scanning and security policies but the volume of uploads makes comprehensive vetting difficult. Attackers exploit misconfigured Docker daemon APIs exposed on the public internet as an initial compromise vector. The vendor has a public vulnerability disclosure program and security team actively addressing these threats.

Docket.history: Docket is a legal technology company founded in 2019 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company provides legal workflow management software. Limited public information is available about its funding history or ownership changes.
Docket.summary_abuse: Docket is a legal technology SaaS with a narrow B2B use case. As a professional services platform requiring business credentials to access, it has minimal public abuse exposure. No documented abuse incidents.

DocsBot.history: DocsBot AI was founded in 2023 by Aaron Edwards of UglyRobot LLC. The platform trains AI chatbots on user documentation and knowledge bases using OpenAI's GPT models to provide automated customer support and documentation search. It has grown to serve businesses ranging from startups to larger enterprises including Sony Network Communications.
DocsBot.summary_abuse: DocsBot AI is a documentation-based AI chatbot SaaS. As an OpenAI API wrapper trained on user-provided documentation, it has potential for misuse if trained on incorrect or misleading content, but the vendor enforces terms of service. No specific abuse incidents have been publicly documented.

Docsify.history: Docsify is an open-source JavaScript documentation site generator that renders Markdown files dynamically without needing to compile static HTML. It was created by Cindy and released in 2016. It gained popularity as a lightweight alternative to GitBook and VuePress for documentation sites.
Docsify.summary_abuse: Docsify is a client-side documentation renderer with no server component or hosting platform. It has no abuse surface as it only generates documentation pages from markdown files. No abuse concerns documented.

Doctave.history: Doctave was founded in 2021 in Helsinki, Finland by Niklas Begley. The company provides a docs-as-code SaaS documentation platform built on Rust, supporting Git-based workflow for authoring and publishing Markdown and API reference documentation. It is a small, independent startup with 2-10 employees.
Doctave.summary_abuse: Doctave is a documentation SaaS for developer and technical teams. As a B2B platform requiring business credentials, it has minimal abuse exposure. No documented abuse incidents.

Doctena.history: Doctena was founded in 2013 in Luxembourg by Alain Fontaine, Patrick Kersten, and Marc Molitor as a European online medical appointment booking platform. In December 2015, the company raised €4.5 million in funding. In November 2016, Doctena acquired Doxter, Germany's leading online medical booking platform. Doctena operates in Luxembourg, Belgium, Netherlands, Switzerland, and Germany.
Doctena.summary_abuse: Doctena is a medical appointment booking platform operating in Europe. As a healthcare scheduling service, it is subject to GDPR and handles patient booking data. No documented abuse incidents have been publicly reported. The platform serves medical professionals and patients directly.

Doctor.com.history: Doctor.com was founded in 2012 in New York City as a healthcare marketing and automation platform for medical practices. The company raised venture funding including a $5 million Series A led by Spring Mountain Capital. In October 2020, Doctor.com was acquired by Press Ganey, a Boston-based patient experience company, as part of a push to build the largest healthcare consumerism platform.
Doctor.com.summary_abuse: Doctor.com is a B2B healthcare marketing SaaS now owned by Press Ganey. As a HIPAA-compliant platform handling patient data for healthcare organizations, it operates under strict regulatory requirements. No specific abuse incidents are documented. The platform is used by healthcare providers to manage their digital presence.

Docusaurus.history: Docusaurus is an open-source static site generator created by Meta (Facebook) and first released in 2017 under the MIT license. It was designed to make it easy to build documentation websites for open-source projects. Docusaurus 2 was released in 2022 as a complete rewrite using React and became widely adopted across the open-source ecosystem. It is maintained by the Meta Open Source team.
Docusaurus.summary_abuse: Docusaurus is a documentation site generator with no hosted platform or user-generated content capability. It generates static files deployed to arbitrary hosting. No abuse concerns are attributed to the tool itself.

DocuSign.history: DocuSign was founded in 2003 by Court Lorenzini, Barry Frischling, and Eric Ranft in San Francisco, California. It became the dominant electronic signature platform globally. The company went public via IPO in April 2018 on NASDAQ. DocuSign has faced competition from Adobe Sign and HelloSign (acquired by Dropbox). It underwent significant executive changes in 2022 including CEO departures. The company rebranded its broader offering as the Intelligent Agreement Management (IAM) platform in 2024.
DocuSign.summary_abuse: DocuSign is a widely abused brand in phishing campaigns. Attackers frequently impersonate DocuSign with spoofed emails and fake signature request notifications to steal credentials or deliver malware. DocuSign itself is not compromised in these attacks; the brand is exploited due to its ubiquity. The company actively publishes alerts about phishing campaigns using its name and maintains a reporting mechanism. Legitimate DocuSign usage is widespread across enterprises for legally binding document signing.

Dojo.history: Dojo Toolkit was created by Alex Russell, Dylan Schiemann, David Schontzler, and collaborators, with its first release on April 24, 2004. The Dojo Foundation was formed in January 2005. In 2016, the Dojo Foundation merged with the jQuery Foundation to form the JS Foundation, which in 2019 merged with the Node.js Foundation to become the OpenJS Foundation. Dojo was an influential pioneer in JavaScript library design and is now under OpenJS Foundation stewardship.
Dojo.summary_abuse: Dojo Toolkit is a client-side JavaScript library with no server component or hosting capability. It has no abuse surface and no documented abuse incidents. It is foundational technology used in enterprise web applications.

Dokan.history: Dokan is a multivendor marketplace plugin for WooCommerce developed by weDevs, a Bangladeshi company. The plugin was first released in September 2014 and has grown to over 60,000 active installs. weDevs is based in Dhaka, Bangladesh and also develops WP User Frontend and other WordPress plugins.
Dokan.summary_abuse: Dokan is a WooCommerce multivendor marketplace plugin. Marketplace plugins can facilitate fraudulent vendor setups if site administrators lack proper KYC for vendors. Abuse risk is low for the plugin itself and primarily depends on how site operators manage their marketplace. No systemic abuse issues specific to Dokan have been documented.

Dokeos.history: Dokeos is an open-source e-learning and LMS platform that originated as a fork of Claroline in 2004, developed by a Belgian company. It provided online course creation and learning management functionality for educational institutions and businesses. The project was largely superseded by its own fork, Chamilo, which was created in 2010. Dokeos has seen limited development activity since then.
Dokeos.summary_abuse: Dokeos is a self-hosted LMS platform with minimal abuse exposure. As an educational platform requiring user registration, it is not commonly targeted for abuse. No significant abuse incidents have been documented.

DokuWiki.history: DokuWiki was created by Andreas Gohr in 2004 as a simple, file-based wiki software that requires no database. It is written in PHP and released under the GPL v2 license. DokuWiki is distinguished by its flat-file storage approach, making it easy to install and maintain. It has been maintained primarily by Gohr as an independent project for over 20 years and has developed a large plugin ecosystem.
DokuWiki.summary_abuse: DokuWiki is a self-hosted wiki platform. Publicly accessible DokuWiki installations without proper access controls have been targeted for spam page injection and SEO manipulation. The risk depends on whether the installation allows anonymous editing. The vendor provides security advisories. No systemic vendor-side abuse issues.

Dolead.history: Dolead was founded in 2007 in Paris, France by Guillaume Heintz and Arthur Saint-Pere. The company specializes in paid marketing campaign management and exclusive lead generation for B2C businesses, integrating customer data for sales-ready leads. Dolead has offices in Paris and Boston.
Dolead.summary_abuse: Dolead is a B2B lead generation and digital marketing SaaS. As a marketing data platform, it handles significant amounts of consumer data for targeting purposes, which carries privacy implications. The company is subject to GDPR as a French company. No specific fraud or abuse incidents have been publicly documented.

DomainFactory.history: DomainFactory was founded in 1999 in Munich, Germany as one of the largest German web hosting providers. The company grew to manage over 1.3 million domains and serve 220,000+ customers. It was acquired by Host Europe Group (HEG), which in turn was acquired by GoDaddy in 2017, making DomainFactory a GoDaddy subsidiary operating in the German market.
DomainFactory.summary_abuse: DomainFactory is a German web hosting provider now owned by GoDaddy. As a hosting provider, its infrastructure can be used to host malicious content including phishing sites. In 2018, DomainFactory suffered a data breach affecting customer data. The vendor complies with German data protection law and GDPR. Abuse takedown responsiveness is typical of large shared hosting providers.

Dominate WooCommerce.history: Dominate WooCommerce (woocommerce-domination) is a small open-source WordPress plugin that reorganizes the WordPress admin interface to prioritize WooCommerce menus and controls. It was created by Claudio Sanches and released on WordPress.org. The plugin has had limited ongoing maintenance.
Dominate WooCommerce.summary_abuse: Dominate WooCommerce is a simple WordPress admin UI plugin with no public-facing surface. It has no abuse risk or documented security issues.

Donorbox.history: Donorbox was founded in 2014 by Charles Zhang in San Francisco, California. The platform provides embedded donation forms and fundraising tools for nonprofits, now serving over 100,000 organizations across 40+ countries. The company has grown steadily as a bootstrapped, profitable company without taking institutional VC funding.
Donorbox.summary_abuse: Donorbox is a nonprofit donation platform used by over 100,000 organizations. As a payment-processing service handling donor funds, it maintains PCI-DSS compliance and SOC 2 Type 2 certification. Fraudulent donation campaigns or charity scams could theoretically exploit the platform, but Donorbox verifies nonprofit status and enforces fraud prevention policies. The platform monitors for suspicious activity.

DonorPerfect.history: DonorPerfect was first developed by Starkland Systems in 1987 in California. SofterWare, Inc., founded by Nathan Relles and Doug Schoenberg in 1981, began distributing DonorPerfect in 1989. SofterWare acquired Starkland Systems in 2002, becoming the sole owner and developer of DonorPerfect. The platform became a leading fundraising and donor management solution for nonprofits, now serving over 11,000 organizations.
DonorPerfect.summary_abuse: DonorPerfect is a nonprofit fundraising and donor management SaaS used by over 11,000 organizations. It processes donation payments and handles donor PII, making data protection important. The software is PCI-DSS compliant for payment handling. No significant abuse incidents have been publicly documented.

Dooca.history: Dooca Commerce is a Brazilian e-commerce platform founded in 2014 and headquartered in Novo Hamburgo, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. The company provides SaaS e-commerce solutions for Brazilian small and medium-sized businesses. It has grown to employ 51-200 people.
Dooca.summary_abuse: Dooca is a Brazilian e-commerce SaaS platform targeting SMBs. As a hosted storefront solution, stores built on Dooca could potentially be used to host fraudulent storefronts, though the platform enforces terms of service. The platform operates primarily within the Brazilian market and is subject to Brazilian consumer protection law (LGPD).

Doofinder.history: Doofinder was founded in 2010 in Madrid, Spain as a SaaS site search solution for e-commerce and online stores. The company bootstrapped to serve over 10,000 clients globally, offering AI-powered search integration for major e-commerce platforms including WooCommerce, Shopify, Magento, and PrestaShop. It remains independently owned with no venture capital funding.
Doofinder.summary_abuse: Doofinder is an e-commerce site search SaaS with minimal abuse exposure. As a search widget embedded on e-commerce sites, it collects search analytics data from site visitors, which carries standard privacy considerations. The vendor complies with GDPR as a Spanish company. No documented abuse incidents.

Doorbell.history: Doorbell.io is a small independent SaaS feedback widget used by over 3,000 teams for in-app user feedback collection on web, iOS, and Android. The company is a small team with no notable funding rounds or ownership changes. The product integrates with project management tools like Trello, Asana, and Slack. No major rebrands, acquisitions, or controversies are documented.
Doorbell.summary_abuse: Doorbell.io is a lightweight feedback widget with no notable abuse signal. The tool is embedded in legitimate web and mobile apps to collect user feedback and does not provide public hosting, file sharing, or URL redirection features that are commonly exploited. No documented incidents of abuse or misuse have been reported.

DoorLoop.history: DoorLoop was founded in 2019 by David Bitton, Ori Tamuz, Itay Gardi, and Matthew Cave as a property management software platform for landlords and property managers. The company raised $10M in seed funding in September 2021, followed by a $20M Series A in 2022 led by ASG, and a $100M Series B in October 2025, bringing total funding to approximately $182M. No acquisitions or major controversies noted.
DoorLoop.summary_abuse: DoorLoop is a B2B property management SaaS with no notable abuse signal. The platform serves landlords and property managers and does not provide consumer-facing public hosting or file-sharing surfaces that are typically exploited by bad actors. No documented incidents of phishing, malware hosting, or scam usage have been reported.

Doppler.history: Doppler was founded in 2018 in San Francisco by Brian Vallelunga and Jack Legg after Vallelunga experienced the pain of secrets management while building a personal project. The company was part of Y Combinator's W19 batch and has raised $28.9M from investors including Sequoia Capital, Google, CRV, and Y Combinator. Doppler provides a developer-first secrets management platform that centralizes environment variables and secrets across teams and cloud providers.
Doppler.summary_abuse: Doppler is a secrets management platform used by development teams to manage API keys and environment variables. It has no notable abuse signal as it is a backend infrastructure tool with no public-facing hosting surface. The platform enforces access controls and audit logging. No documented incidents of abuse or misuse have been reported.

Doppler for WooCommerce.history: Doppler for WooCommerce is a free WordPress plugin developed by Doppler (fromdoppler.com), an email marketing company founded in Argentina around 2006. The plugin synchronizes WooCommerce buyers and contacts to Doppler email lists and includes on-site tracking functionality for cart abandonment tracking. The plugin is maintained on GitHub at FromDoppler/doppler-woocommerce-plugin with regular updates. No major controversies or ownership changes documented.
Doppler for WooCommerce.summary_abuse: Doppler for WooCommerce is a WordPress plugin with low abuse risk. It syncs WooCommerce customer data to the Doppler email marketing platform and includes on-site behavioral tracking. The tracking script functionality means it collects visitor behavior data, which could be a privacy concern if not disclosed properly. No documented incidents of the plugin being used maliciously.

Doppler Forms.history: Doppler Forms is a free WordPress plugin developed by Doppler (fromdoppler.com), an email marketing platform founded in Argentina around 2006. The plugin allows WordPress site owners to create subscription forms that integrate with the Doppler email marketing service. It has been available on the WordPress plugin repository for over a decade with periodic updates. No major ownership changes or controversies are documented.
Doppler Forms.summary_abuse: Doppler Forms is a WordPress subscription form plugin with minimal abuse risk. It is a standard email opt-in tool that connects to the Doppler email marketing platform. No documented incidents of the plugin being used for malicious purposes, though as with all email collection forms it could theoretically be used to build contact lists without clear consent if configured improperly.

Dorik AI.history: Dorik was founded in 2020 by Mizanur Rahman in Middletown, Delaware, with the goal of making professional website creation accessible to non-technical users. The platform offers AI-powered website generation and a no-code builder with unlimited websites. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Dorik AI.summary_abuse: Dorik AI is a website builder that allows users to create and host websites. Like most website builders, it can be used to create phishing pages or scam sites if the vendor's abuse monitoring is insufficient. The free tier with hosted domains represents some abuse risk typical of website builders. No specific documented incidents of widespread abuse have been reported.

Dorsa Cloud.history: Dorsa Cloud is an Iranian cloud technology company providing infrastructure and software services. The company is associated with Dorsa Expert System PJS, which holds AS205134 and operates in Iran's technology sector. Limited public information is available about the company's founding history and team.
Dorsa Cloud.summary_abuse: Dorsa Cloud is a small Iranian cloud services provider with limited public profile. No specific documented abuse incidents are available. The company's Iranian jurisdiction may limit visibility into its operations and compliance posture from a Western regulatory standpoint.

Dotclear.history: Dotclear is a French open-source blog publishing application created by Olivier Meunier in 2002 and released publicly in 2003. The project gathered a small community of contributors and became popular particularly in French-speaking countries where it is used by several major blogging platforms. Originally released under GPLv2, Dotclear updated its license to AGPLv3 in March 2024. The project migrated its source hosting from GitHub to Codeberg in 2022 to align with non-profit, open-source hosting principles.
Dotclear.summary_abuse: Dotclear is a self-hosted open-source blogging platform with no notable abuse signal as a technology. As a self-hosted CMS, any potential abuse would occur at the hosting level rather than through the software itself. The project has no vendor-controlled hosting surface. No documented incidents of widespread abuse through the platform have been reported.

Dotdigital.history: Dotdigital originated as Ellipsis Media in 1999, a UK web design agency that developed the dotmailer email marketing product in 2002. The company rebranded to Dotdigital Group plc and listed on the AIM market of the London Stock Exchange. Over the years the company made several acquisitions: Comapi in 2017 for omnichannel messaging, Fresh Relevance in 2023 for personalization, and Social Snowball in 2025 for influencer marketing for $35M. The platform serves e-commerce brands globally.
Dotdigital.summary_abuse: Dotdigital is an email and SMS marketing automation platform. As a bulk email sender, Dotdigital can be implicated in spam campaigns if customers misuse the platform, though the company enforces anti-spam policies and sender reputation standards. The platform primarily serves legitimate B2C and e-commerce marketers. Dotdigital has standard anti-spam measures in place including consent verification and list management controls.

Dotdigital Chat.history: Dotdigital Chat is a live chat product launched by Dotdigital Group plc in 2020 as an integrated channel within its cross-channel marketing automation platform. The product supports webchat, SMS chat, and WhatsApp chat channels and integrates with Dotdigital's email marketing and CRM features. It is bundled with the Dotdigital platform rather than sold as a standalone product.
Dotdigital Chat.summary_abuse: Dotdigital Chat is a live chat widget operated by Dotdigital, a publicly listed UK marketing automation company. The product is deployed on e-commerce and B2C brand websites and poses minimal abuse risk. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Doteasy.history: Doteasy Technology Inc. was founded in 2000 in Burnaby, British Columbia, Canada, offering affordable shared web hosting and domain registration services primarily for Canadian customers. The company operated independently for over two decades before being acquired by World Host Group in November 2023. Doteasy continues to operate under its original brand serving customers from Vancouver, BC data centers.
Doteasy.summary_abuse: Doteasy is a shared web hosting provider that, like most hosting services, can be used to host malicious content if inadequate monitoring is in place. Shared hosting providers are occasionally used for phishing pages or spam sites. Doteasy is a small Canadian provider with no notable documented abuse incidents, though shared hosting inherently carries moderate abuse potential relative to dedicated enterprise hosting.

Doteasy Website Builder.history: Doteasy Website Builder is a no-code website creation tool offered as part of Doteasy Technology Inc.'s web hosting suite, which has operated since 2000 from Burnaby, British Columbia, Canada. The builder was added to complement Doteasy's hosting plans and allow customers without technical knowledge to create websites. Doteasy was acquired by World Host Group in November 2023.
Doteasy Website Builder.summary_abuse: Doteasy Website Builder is a drag-and-drop website builder bundled with Doteasy hosting. Like most hosted website builders, it could theoretically be used to create phishing or scam sites, but no specific documented incidents have been reported for this particular platform. Doteasy's small customer base limits overall abuse exposure.

DotGo.history: Dotgo is an RCS (Rich Communication Services) messaging platform founded around 2013 and based in New Jersey. The company built the world's first Bot Store for RCS bots and developed the RBM Hub product for carriers. Dotgo was acquired by Gupshup, an Indian conversational messaging company, in 2021. Under Gupshup ownership, Dotgo continues to operate as a key RCS infrastructure provider handling over 1 billion RCS messages globally.
DotGo.summary_abuse: Dotgo is an enterprise RCS messaging infrastructure provider used by carriers and large businesses. The platform operates at the carrier level with enterprise-grade vetting and does not offer self-service sign-up. No notable abuse incidents have been documented. As a messaging platform, theoretical SMS/RCS spam risks exist but are mitigated by the carrier-level deployment model.

DotPe.history: DotPe was founded in March 2020 by Shailaz Nag, Gyanesh Sharma, and Anurag Gupta, all former PayU India employees, to provide digital ordering and payment solutions for offline-to-online merchant transformation. The company raised $27.5 million in Series A funding in March 2021, led by PayU with Google as an investor. DotPe is headquartered in Gurugram, India and focuses on the food and beverage and retail sectors.
DotPe.summary_abuse: DotPe is an Indian digital commerce platform for merchants, primarily focused on payments and online ordering. As a payments platform operating in India, it is subject to RBI regulations. No documented incidents of widespread abuse have been reported. The platform is targeted at legitimate SMB merchants rather than consumer-facing hosting.

Dotser.history: Dotser is an Irish web design and cloud business software company based in Tullamore, County Offaly, founded around 2000. The company provides CMS, eCommerce solutions, and cloud-based business software to clients across Ireland, the UK, Australia, New Zealand, and USA. Dotser has built a reputation particularly in Ireland for club membership management systems and integrated retail software.
Dotser.summary_abuse: Dotser is a small Irish B2B software company focused on web design and business software. The platform is used for legitimate business operations and has no notable abuse signal. The company's small scale and B2B focus minimize exposure to fraudulent use cases.

Dotter.history: Dotter is a Swedish e-commerce technology company offering a Commerce Builder platform that powers digital shopfronts for brands and retailers. The company focuses on multi-retailer digital shelf solutions with real-time pricing and availability data. Limited public information is available about the company's founding date and team.
Dotter.summary_abuse: Dotter is a B2B e-commerce platform for retail brands with no notable abuse signal. The platform serves brand manufacturers and retailers for legitimate commerce operations and does not provide consumer-facing hosting surfaces that could be exploited.

Douban.history: Douban was launched on March 6, 2005, by Yang Bo, a former IBM researcher and computational physics PhD from UC San Diego, initially as a book and movie review site at a Beijing Starbucks. The platform expanded into a comprehensive Chinese social network for culture, arts, and communities. Douban has faced intermittent censorship pressures from Chinese authorities, including a significant crackdown in 2021 when regulators targeted online communities for content violations, resulting in restrictions and fines. The company has pursued IPO plans but had not completed a public listing as of 2026.
Douban.summary_abuse: Douban is a Chinese cultural social network subject to Chinese government content censorship and data regulations. User data is subject to Chinese law and may be shared with government authorities on demand. The platform is not GDPR-compliant and is inaccessible or restricted outside China. Occasional content moderation controversies have arisen related to politically sensitive discussions. Not suitable for enterprise deployments requiring Western privacy standards.

DoubleClick Ad Exchange (AdX).history: DoubleClick Ad Exchange (AdX) originated with DoubleClick Inc., founded in 1996 as an ad serving company. Google acquired DoubleClick in 2007 for $3.1 billion, triggering regulatory scrutiny. Google launched the DoubleClick Ad Exchange in 2009 and by 2014 it became the world's leading ad exchange with 53% market share. In 2018, Google rebranded and consolidated its ad products: DoubleClick Ad Exchange became a feature within Google Ad Manager. In April 2025, a US federal court ruled that Google illegally monopolized the publisher ad server and ad exchange markets.
DoubleClick Ad Exchange (AdX).summary_abuse: DoubleClick Ad Exchange is a massive ad marketplace that has faced significant regulatory scrutiny. As the dominant programmatic advertising exchange, it has been implicated in serving malvertising campaigns where malicious ads are injected into legitimate publisher inventory. Google takes abuse reports seriously and has sophisticated automated fraud detection, but the scale of the platform means some bad-faith advertising still flows through. The platform is central to ongoing antitrust proceedings related to Google's monopoly in ad tech.

DoubleClick Campaign Manager (DCM).history: DoubleClick Campaign Manager (DCM) originated with DoubleClick Inc., founded in 1996 as an ad server. Google acquired DoubleClick in 2007 for $3.1 billion. DCM served as the ad serving and campaign measurement platform for advertisers. In 2018, Google rebranded the entire DoubleClick suite: DCM became Campaign Manager 360 (CM360) under the Google Marketing Platform umbrella. The product is now a cornerstone of enterprise digital advertising for agencies and large advertisers globally.
DoubleClick Campaign Manager (DCM).summary_abuse: DoubleClick Campaign Manager is an enterprise ad serving platform used by major advertising agencies and brands. As an ad serving tool, it is used in conjunction with display and video ad delivery. It does not provide public hosting or content submission surfaces. Abuse risk is limited to potential misuse of ad tracking pixels on publishers' sites, which is standard ad-tech practice. No notable documented abuse incidents outside of standard ad-tech privacy concerns.

DoubleClick Floodlight.history: DoubleClick Floodlight is a conversion tracking system that originated with DoubleClick Inc., founded in 1996, and was part of the DoubleClick suite Google acquired in 2007. Floodlight tags are HTML-based conversion tracking pixels deployed on advertiser websites to measure post-click and post-impression conversions. In 2018, Floodlight became an integral part of Google Marketing Platform, serving as the shared conversion tracking backbone across Campaign Manager 360, Display and Video 360, and Search Ads 360.
DoubleClick Floodlight.summary_abuse: DoubleClick Floodlight is a server-side conversion tracking technology used by enterprise advertisers. It operates as a tracking pixel embedded on advertiser websites to measure conversions. As ad-tech tracking infrastructure, Floodlight collects detailed user behavior data across sites for attribution purposes, which raises standard privacy concerns. It is used only by vetted advertisers through Google Marketing Platform. No documented incidents of Floodlight being weaponized by bad actors have been reported.

DoubleClick for Publishers (DFP).history: DoubleClick for Publishers (DFP) was the ad server product from DoubleClick Inc., founded in 1996. After Google's $3.1 billion acquisition of DoubleClick in 2007, Google continued developing DFP as the primary ad server for publishers. DFP Small Business was offered free to smaller publishers. In 2018, Google rebranded the entire DoubleClick product suite: DFP became Google Ad Manager. In April 2025, a US federal court ruled Google illegally monopolized the publisher ad server market, with DFP/Google Ad Manager central to this ruling.
DoubleClick for Publishers (DFP).summary_abuse: DoubleClick for Publishers is Google's dominant publisher ad server used by the majority of large publishers globally. As an ad serving platform, it can serve malvertising if malicious ads bypass Google's vetting. Google operates sophisticated automated abuse detection, but the scale means some fraudulent or low-quality ads still reach consumers. The platform's market dominance has been at the center of antitrust litigation related to Google's control of ad tech infrastructure.

DoubleTick.history: DoubleTick was founded in 2022 by Deepak Bhagchandani in Mumbai, India, as a WhatsApp Business API platform for merchants. The company is an Official Meta Technology Partner and is ISO 27001 and GDPR certified. DoubleTick targets B2B, B2C, and D2C businesses in India for customer engagement via WhatsApp. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
DoubleTick.summary_abuse: DoubleTick is a WhatsApp Business API platform enabling bulk messaging and chatbot automation. As a WhatsApp marketing tool, there is inherent risk of being used for unsolicited bulk messaging (spam) if customer lists are not properly consented. DoubleTick is an Official Meta Technology Partner which requires adherence to WhatsApp Business policies. No specific documented abuse incidents have been reported, though bulk messaging platforms in general carry moderate spam risk.

DoubleVerify.history: DoubleVerify was co-founded in March 2008 in New York by Oren Netzer and Alex Galai to provide independent verification of digital ad delivery, viewability, and brand safety. The company raised a $33M Series C in 2011 and made strategic acquisitions including Leiki, Ad-Juster, and OpenSlate between 2018 and 2021. DoubleVerify went public on the NYSE in April 2021, raising approximately $360M at a roughly $4.5 billion valuation. Annual revenue reached $657M in 2024.
DoubleVerify.summary_abuse: DoubleVerify is an ad verification and brand safety platform whose purpose is to reduce abuse in digital advertising, including fraud detection and brand safety controls. As anti-abuse infrastructure, the platform itself is not subject to typical abuse patterns. DoubleVerify technology is embedded via tags on publisher and advertiser infrastructure. No documented incidents of DoubleVerify being misused by bad actors have been reported.

Dover.history: Dover was founded in 2019 by George Carollo, Anvisha Pai, and Max Kolysh and was part of Y Combinator's Summer 2019 batch. The company raised $20 million in funding and built an AI-powered recruiting orchestration platform that helps startups source candidates from platforms like LinkedIn, Indeed, and Triplebyte. Dover has helped over 1,500 companies including Stripe, Scale AI, and Vanta hire talent. The company offers a free ATS alongside its paid recruiting services.
Dover.summary_abuse: Dover is a recruiting software platform with no notable abuse signal. The tool is used by legitimate companies to source and manage job candidates. Access to candidate data requires verifying legitimate company use. No documented incidents of the platform being used for fraudulent recruitment, social engineering, or data harvesting have been reported.

Dovetale.history: Dovetale was founded in 2017 by Mike Schmidt and Navied Shoushtarian as an influencer marketing platform, one of the first graduates of Expa Labs. The platform grew to serve over 650 marketing teams globally. Shopify acquired Dovetale on April 13, 2022, and subsequently rebranded and integrated the product into Shopify Collabs in August 2022, offering influencer and creator management to Shopify merchants.
Dovetale.summary_abuse: Dovetale, now operating as Shopify Collabs, is an influencer marketing platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform is used to manage brand-influencer relationships and is now part of Shopify's merchant suite. As a Shopify subsidiary, it benefits from Shopify's enterprise security and compliance posture. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Download Monitor.history: Download Monitor is a popular WordPress plugin that provides tools to manage and track file downloads on WordPress sites. The plugin has been available on the WordPress.org plugin repository for over a decade and is maintained by WPChill. The free version provides core download management functionality, while the premium version sold at download-monitor.com adds advanced reporting and access controls.
Download Monitor.summary_abuse: Download Monitor is a WordPress file download management plugin with no notable abuse signal. The plugin manages downloads of legitimate files on WordPress sites and does not itself provide any public-facing file hosting that could be exploited. No documented security incidents have been reported for this plugin.

doxo.history: doxo was founded in 2008 and is a US-based consumer bill payment aggregator service. It allows consumers to pay multiple bills through one platform. The service has faced criticism and FTC scrutiny over deceptive practices related to misleading consumers about official payment channels.
doxo.summary_abuse: doxo has been the subject of FTC investigation for practices that could mislead consumers into believing they are paying bills directly to utilities or service providers, while doxo adds fees. The service has been criticized for dark patterns in its billing presentation. It processes consumer payments but has notable controversy around transparency of fees and its positioning as an intermediary.

Doxygen.history: Doxygen was created by Dimitri van Heesch and first publicly released in 1997. It borrowed initial code from DOC++, a documentation tool from Zuse Institute Berlin. Written in C++, Doxygen became the de facto standard tool for generating documentation from annotated source code and supports C++, C, Java, Python, PHP, and many others. The project has been continuously developed for nearly three decades with active releases maintained on GitHub.
Doxygen.summary_abuse: Doxygen is a documentation generation tool with zero abuse signal. It is a developer utility that runs locally on source code to generate documentation files and has no network service, user accounts, or consumer-facing surface.

DPD.history: DPD (Deutscher Paketdienst) was founded in 1976 by 18 German forwarding companies. In 2001, GeoPost (the express parcel division of France's La Poste Groupe) acquired a majority stake. In 2015 GeoPost unified its DPD, Chronopost and Seur brands under the DPDgroup umbrella. In 2023, GeoPost/DPDgroup rebranded as Geopost.
DPD.summary_abuse: DPD is a major international parcel carrier. Its tracking infrastructure and delivery notifications are occasionally spoofed in phishing SMS/email campaigns (fake delivery-fee scams), but this involves impersonation of DPD rather than abuse of DPD's own platform. DPD has a published AUP and large-scale fraud monitoring as part of La Poste Groupe. Abuse is minimal relative to legitimate parcel delivery volume.

DPlayer.history: DPlayer is an open-source HTML5 danmaku video player created by Chinese developer DIYgod, popular in Chinese tech communities. It was released on GitHub under the MoePlayer organization and supports danmaku (bullet comment) overlays, multiple video formats, and streaming protocols. The project is MIT-licensed.
DPlayer.summary_abuse: DPlayer is an open-source client-side video player library. It has no server-side or hosted surface and presents no direct abuse risk, though it could be embedded in pages hosting pirated content as a player component.

Dr. Leonardo.history: Dr. Leonardo is an eHealth SaaS website builder for healthcare practices, evolving from MedNet Technologies, which was founded in 1999 and acquired in 2015. The platform has over 20 years of experience building healthcare websites and transitioned to a SaaS automated website builder model. Dr. Leonardo is offered as a white-label enterprise solution for healthcare organizations.
Dr. Leonardo.summary_abuse: Dr. Leonardo is a healthcare-focused SaaS website builder with no notable abuse signal. The platform serves medical, dental, and other healthcare practitioners and is designed for legitimate professional use. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Draft.js.history: Draft.js was developed by Facebook and open-sourced in February 2016 as a React-based rich text editor framework. It powered Facebook's own rich text features including status updates, comments, and Messenger. The library gained rapid adoption in the React ecosystem. In 2022, Facebook moved Draft.js to the facebookarchive organization on GitHub, signaling it is no longer actively maintained. Meta released Lexical as its successor.
Draft.js.summary_abuse: Draft.js is a client-side JavaScript library with no abuse signal. It is a text editor framework that runs in the browser with no network service or server-side component. No security vulnerabilities or abuse incidents have been associated with the library.

Draftpress HFCM.history: Draftpress HFCM (Header Footer Code Manager) is a WordPress plugin developed by 99robots and later maintained by DraftPress. It allows WordPress site owners to easily add code snippets, scripts, and tracking tags to the header or footer of their site without modifying theme files. The free version is available on WordPress.org and the pro version is sold by DraftPress. The plugin has over 500,000 active installs.
Draftpress HFCM.summary_abuse: Draftpress HFCM is a WordPress plugin used to inject code snippets into website headers and footers. While the plugin itself is legitimate, it can be potentially misused by malicious actors who gain unauthorized admin access to inject tracking scripts or malicious code. No documented incidents of the plugin being used as an attack vector have been widely reported, though any plugin that modifies page output carries inherent risk if a site is compromised.

Dragon.history: Dragon speech recognition was first commercialized by Dragon Systems, founded in 1982 by James and Janet Baker. The product Dragon NaturallySpeaking launched in 1997 as a consumer product. Dragon Systems was acquired by Lernout and Hauspie in 2000, which subsequently went bankrupt. ScanSoft acquired the Dragon assets and rebranded itself as Nuance Communications in 2005. Microsoft acquired Nuance Communications in March 2022 for $19.7 billion, making Dragon a Microsoft product with strong focus on healthcare ambient clinical intelligence.
Dragon.summary_abuse: Dragon is a speech recognition product primarily used by enterprise, healthcare, and productivity users for voice-to-text transcription. It processes voice data locally or in Microsoft cloud infrastructure. No documented incidents of Dragon being used for malicious purposes have been reported. As a Microsoft product, it is subject to Microsoft's security and compliance programs.

Drapr.history: Drapr was founded by David Pastewka, Will Drevno, and Richard Berwick and graduated from Y Combinator in Summer 2020. The startup developed 3D virtual try-on technology that let shoppers create avatars and virtually try on clothing to find their best fit. Drapr was acquired by Gap Inc. in August 2021 for use across its brands including Gap, Old Navy, Banana Republic, and Athleta. The technology was integrated into Gap's digital shopping experiences.
Drapr.summary_abuse: Drapr is a 3D virtual try-on technology platform owned by Gap Inc. and deployed on Gap's retail websites. The technology has no notable abuse signal and is used for legitimate e-commerce size and fit assistance. No documented incidents of abuse or misuse have been reported.

Drata.history: Drata was founded in 2020 by Adam Markowitz, Troy Markowitz, and Daniel Marashlian, who previously built compliance automation tools at their ed-tech company Portfolium. Drata raised over $300 million in funding, including a $200 million round in December 2022 valuing the company at $2 billion, achieving unicorn status. In September 2024, Drata laid off approximately 9% of its workforce as part of restructuring. The company serves thousands of customers across SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, and over 30 compliance frameworks.
Drata.summary_abuse: Drata is a security compliance automation platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform accesses customer infrastructure to collect compliance evidence via integrations but operates under strict SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001, and other certifications. It is used exclusively by legitimate organizations seeking compliance attestation. No documented incidents of abuse or security breaches at Drata have been reported.

DrChrono.history: DrChrono was founded in 2009 in New York City by Daniel Kivatinos and Michael Nusimow, initially incubated at Rose Tech Ventures before joining Y Combinator. The company built an iOS-first EHR platform for medical practices and was one of the first EHRs to support iPad. EverCommerce Inc., a publicly traded vertical SaaS company, acquired DrChrono in November 2021 for approximately $180 million. DrChrono now operates as part of EverCommerce's EverHealth business unit.
DrChrono.summary_abuse: DrChrono is a HIPAA-compliant electronic health records platform used by medical practitioners. The platform handles protected health information (PHI) and operates under strict HIPAA regulations. No notable abuse incidents have been documented. As a healthcare platform, it undergoes regular security audits and is subject to strict regulatory oversight.

DreamApply.history: DreamApply was established in 2011 in Tallinn, Estonia, as a student application management platform for higher education institutions. The company became a de facto national admission system for international students in Estonia and expanded to serve over 300 universities across 40 countries. DreamApply is recognized by Education Estonia and EdTech Estonia as a key Estonian education technology export.
DreamApply.summary_abuse: DreamApply is a student enrollment platform used by legitimate higher education institutions. The platform handles applicant data including academic records and personal information. As an education platform, it is GDPR compliant and used exclusively by vetted universities. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Dreamdata.history: Dreamdata was founded in 2018 in Copenhagen by Lars Grønnegaard, Ole Lerche Dallerup, and Steffen Hedebrandt, all with backgrounds at Trustpilot, Upwork, and Airtame. The company built a B2B revenue attribution and analytics platform. Dreamdata raised a €6M round in 2021 and a €7M Series A in 2022. In October 2025, the company closed a $55M Series B led by PeakSpan Capital, bringing total funding to approximately $67M.
Dreamdata.summary_abuse: Dreamdata is a B2B marketing analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. The tool integrates with CRM, marketing automation, and ad platforms to provide revenue attribution. It processes business customer journey data for legitimate B2B analytics purposes. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

DreamHost.history: DreamHost was founded in 1996 by Dallas Bethune, Josh Jones, Michael Rodriguez, and Sage Weil as undergraduates at Harvey Mudd College in Claremont, California, formally incorporated as New Dream Network LLC in 1997. DreamHost became known for its developer-friendly hosting and opposition to internet surveillance. In 2012, DreamHost spun off Inktank (professional services for Ceph) and in 2014 spun off Akanda (network virtualization). The company remains privately held with approximately 400,000+ customers.
DreamHost.summary_abuse: DreamHost is a shared and managed web hosting provider. Like all hosting services, its shared hosting infrastructure can be used to host phishing pages, malware distribution sites, or spam infrastructure. DreamHost has faced some criticism for being slow to respond to abuse reports compared to larger providers. The company publishes an AUP and processes abuse complaints, but scale limitations mean response times can vary. DreamHost has historically been privacy-conscious and fought government data requests.

DreamROI.history: DreamROI is an eCommerce content personalization platform that tailors on-site shopping experiences based on user behavior and product interactions. The company has limited public profile and historical information. No notable ownership changes or major events are documented.
DreamROI.summary_abuse: DreamROI is an eCommerce personalization tool with no documented abuse signal. The platform operates as a behavioral tracking and personalization layer on merchant websites. No incidents of misuse have been reported.

DreamWeaver.history: Dreamweaver was originally created by Macromedia in 1997, built from the codebase of Backstage (acquired by Macromedia in 1996). It quickly became the leading WYSIWYG HTML editor for web designers. Adobe acquired Macromedia in 2005 for approximately $3.4 billion, bringing Dreamweaver into the Adobe Creative Suite. Adobe integrated Dreamweaver into Creative Cloud in 2014. While still actively sold, Dreamweaver has seen declining relevance as code editors like VS Code and modern frameworks have become dominant among developers.
DreamWeaver.summary_abuse: Dreamweaver is a desktop web design and code editing application owned by Adobe. It is installed locally and has no consumer-facing hosting or content publishing surface that could be exploited. No documented incidents of abuse associated with Dreamweaver have been reported.

DressOn.history: DressOn is a Brazilian AI-powered virtual fitting room platform that lets online shoppers try on clothes digitally. The company is a startup with limited public history. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
DressOn.summary_abuse: DressOn is an AI virtual try-on platform for fashion e-commerce with no notable abuse signal. The platform processes images for clothing visualization and does not involve financial transactions or user-generated content that could be exploited. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Drift.history: Drift was founded in 2015 by David Cancel and Elias Torres to pioneer the conversational marketing category, replacing lead capture forms with real-time live chat. The company grew to power over 50,000 businesses and raised significant venture capital. Salesloft acquired Drift on February 13, 2024, for a reported $500 million, integrating its conversational AI and live chat capabilities into Salesloft's sales engagement platform.
Drift.summary_abuse: Drift is a live chat and conversational AI platform embedded on B2B marketing websites. The chat widget collects visitor data and contact information as part of its lead generation function. The platform has no notable abuse signal beyond standard data collection for marketing purposes. No documented incidents of Drift being weaponized by bad actors have been reported.

Drinks.history: Drinks is a US-based platform providing real-time alcohol tax and regulatory compliance solutions for wineries and retailers selling online. The platform integrates with Shopify to handle the complex regulatory landscape of direct-to-consumer alcohol shipping across US states. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Drinks.summary_abuse: Drinks is a regulatory compliance platform for alcohol retailers with no notable abuse signal. The platform handles tax compliance and age verification for legal alcohol sales. It is used by licensed wineries and retailers operating within regulatory frameworks. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Drip.history: Drip was founded around 2013 and started as a lightweight email opt-in widget before pivoting to a full email marketing automation platform focused on e-commerce. Leadpages acquired Drip in 2016 to complement its landing page product. In 2022, Drip acquired Sleeknote, an on-site engagement platform. Drip has generated over $2 billion in sales for its customers since 2018 and integrates with Shopify, WooCommerce, and Magento.
Drip.summary_abuse: Drip is an email marketing automation platform for e-commerce brands. As a bulk email sender, Drip can be used to send spam if customers upload unconsented contact lists. The company enforces CAN-SPAM and GDPR compliance requirements and has anti-spam measures. Email marketing platforms inherently carry moderate abuse risk as they provide mass email distribution infrastructure.

Droip.history: Droip is a WordPress no-code website builder with drag-and-drop functionality for creating responsive websites. The plugin provides a visual interface for building WordPress sites without coding. Limited public information is available about the company's founding history.
Droip.summary_abuse: Droip is a WordPress website builder plugin. Like other site builder tools, it could be used to create phishing or scam pages if improperly monitored. However, as a self-hosted WordPress plugin, abuse occurs at the hosting level rather than through Droip itself. No documented incidents of Droip-specific abuse have been reported.

Drop A Hint.history: Drop A Hint is a Shopify app that enables e-commerce shoppers to share product wishlists and hints via email, SMS, WhatsApp, and other messengers. It is a small independent Shopify app developer with limited public history. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Drop A Hint.summary_abuse: Drop A Hint is a Shopify gift hinting app with minimal abuse risk. The app facilitates sharing product links via messaging, which could theoretically be used for spam, but its B2C retail context limits meaningful abuse exposure. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Dropbox.history: Dropbox was founded in 2007 by Drew Houston and Arash Ferdowsi, MIT students, with seed funding from Y Combinator. Drew Houston conceived the idea after forgetting his USB flash drive. The company launched publicly in 2008 with a viral referral program that drove explosive growth. Dropbox went public on the Nasdaq (DBX) on March 23, 2018, raising $756 million at a $12 billion valuation. The company has over 700 million registered users and 17 million paid users.
Dropbox.summary_abuse: Dropbox is a major file storage and sharing platform that is regularly abused for malicious file hosting and distribution. Security researchers have documented Dropbox links being used to distribute malware, ransomware, and phishing kits, as legitimate Dropbox domains are often trusted by enterprise security filters. Dropbox has anti-abuse measures and responds to takedown requests, but the free tier with public sharing remains a vector for malware hosting. The platform is favored by threat actors for staging payloads because Dropbox CDN domains bypass many security filters.

DropInBlog.history: DropInBlog was started around 2013 as a side project by Jesse Schoberg and his co-founder, launching publicly in 2015. The platform fills a niche for website owners on non-WordPress platforms who want SEO-optimized blogs without switching CMS. The company gained significant traction after 2019 when the co-founders focused full time, eventually reaching seven-figure annual revenue. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
DropInBlog.summary_abuse: DropInBlog is a SaaS blogging platform with no notable abuse signal. The platform is used by legitimate website owners to add blogging capabilities to e-commerce and other sites. No documented incidents of the platform being used for malicious content hosting have been reported.

Droplabs.history: Droplabs is a Polish ticketing and dynamic pricing platform for tourist attractions, amusement parks, aquaparks, museums, and event organizers. The company is Poland's leading online sales software for the experiences industry. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Droplabs.summary_abuse: Droplabs is a B2B ticketing platform for tourist attractions and events with no notable abuse signal. The platform is used by legitimate venues for ticket sales and access control. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Dropzone.history: Dropzone.js is an open source JavaScript library created by Matias Meno, initially released around 2012. It provides drag-and-drop file upload functionality with image previews and progress bars. The library became widely popular for adding file upload UX to web applications and is available as an npm package. The project has undergone maintenance transfers and continues to be used in millions of web applications.
Dropzone.summary_abuse: Dropzone.js is a client-side JavaScript library with no abuse signal. It runs in the browser and handles the UI for file uploads. Any abuse would occur at the server level receiving the uploads, not through the library itself. No security vulnerabilities have been widely documented for this library.

Droxit.history: Droxit is an automated web accessibility solution that helps websites comply with accessibility standards. The company has limited public profile and history. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Droxit.summary_abuse: Droxit is a web accessibility compliance tool with no notable abuse signal. The product is used by legitimate website operators to improve accessibility. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Droxy.history: Droxy is a no-code AI chatbot platform founded around 2023 that allows businesses to build and deploy customizable chatbots and agents. The platform uses large language models including ChatGPT to create knowledge-based chatbots from PDFs, websites, and videos. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Droxy.summary_abuse: Droxy is an AI chatbot builder platform with moderate abuse risk typical of AI wrapper products. The platform could be used to create deceptive or phishing chatbots if misused. As a relatively new AI product with no-code deployment, it carries typical risks associated with chatbot platforms. No specific documented abuse incidents have been reported.

Droz Bot.history: Droz Bot is a Brazilian multi-channel chatbot platform providing automated customer service across Instagram, WhatsApp, and other messaging apps. The company operates from Brazil and targets businesses seeking 24/7 automated customer engagement. Limited public history is available.
Droz Bot.summary_abuse: Droz Bot is a Brazilian chatbot platform for customer service automation with no notable documented abuse signal. The platform is designed for legitimate business customer communication. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Drubbit.history: Drubbit is a small e-commerce platform with CMS features for product pages, checkout management, payments, and shipping. The company has limited public profile and history. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Drubbit.summary_abuse: Drubbit is a small e-commerce platform with limited public profile. Like most hosted commerce platforms, it carries moderate theoretical abuse risk if used to create fraudulent storefronts. No specific documented abuse incidents have been reported.

Drupal.history: Drupal was created by Dries Buytaert in 2000 as a message board and released as open source on January 15, 2001. Interest grew significantly in 2003 when it powered the DeanSpace community for Howard Dean's presidential campaign. Dries Buytaert co-founded Acquia in 2007 as a commercial support company for Drupal. The Drupal Association was established to support the Drupal community and software. As of 2017, over 1.18 million websites use Drupal, including major government sites, universities, and corporations. In April 2023, Drupal was recognized by the UN as a Digital Public Good.
Drupal.summary_abuse: Drupal is a widely used open-source CMS with moderate abuse exposure. Because Drupal powers millions of websites, known vulnerabilities (when disclosed) can be exploited at scale before patches are applied, as seen in major incidents like Drupalgeddon2 in 2018 where hundreds of thousands of sites were compromised. Drupal's security team maintains an active patch and disclosure process. Outdated Drupal installations are frequently targeted for backdoor installation and malicious redirect injection.
