Drupal Commerce.history: Drupal Commerce was created by Ryan Szrama and Commerce Guys (now Centarro) and released in 2011 as an e-commerce framework built on Drupal. The project emerged from the Ubercart e-commerce module for Drupal and aimed to create a more flexible, API-first commerce solution. Commerce Guys rebranded to Centarro in 2019. The project has strong enterprise adoption and is used for complex B2B and B2C commerce scenarios globally.
Drupal Commerce.summary_abuse: Drupal Commerce is an open-source e-commerce framework built on Drupal with no notable standalone abuse signal. Any abuse risk is inherent to the Drupal CMS platform rather than Commerce specifically, and relates to vulnerabilities in outdated Drupal installations. The module itself is used by legitimate businesses for complex commerce use cases. No documented incidents specific to Drupal Commerce being exploited for fraudulent storefronts have been reported.

Drupal Multisite.history: Drupal Multisite is a core feature of the Drupal CMS that allows multiple websites to run from a single Drupal installation, sharing code but with separate databases and configurations. This feature has been part of Drupal since its early versions (released from 2001). It is particularly popular with universities, government agencies, and large enterprises that need to manage many related sites. No separate founding or ownership history exists as it is an integral feature of the Drupal core project.
Drupal Multisite.summary_abuse: Drupal Multisite is a configuration approach for self-hosted Drupal installations. Abuse risk is inherited from the Drupal platform and is not amplified by multisite configurations. Outdated Drupal installations in multisite setups can be particularly high-value targets for attackers since one vulnerability can compromise multiple sites simultaneously. No Drupal Multisite-specific abuse incidents have been documented separately from general Drupal exploits.

DSpace.history: DSpace was developed collaboratively by MIT Libraries and Hewlett-Packard Labs and released publicly in November 2002. In 2007, HP and MIT formed the not-for-profit DSpace Foundation to support the growing user community. In 2009, the DSpace Foundation merged with Fedora Commons to form DuraSpace. In July 2019, DuraSpace merged with LYRASIS, which now provides stewardship for the project. DSpace powers over 3,000 institutional repositories worldwide.
DSpace.summary_abuse: DSpace is an open-source institutional repository platform used by universities and research institutions worldwide to archive scholarly content. It has no notable abuse signal. Its use is overwhelmingly legitimate, centered on long-term preservation of academic and institutional digital content.

DTScout.history: DTScout was founded in 2014 in Tel Aviv, Israel as a marketing data intelligence and audience discovery platform aggregating behavioral and demographic data from publishers. The company remains a small independent operation with fewer than 10 employees.
DTScout.summary_abuse: DTScout aggregates audience behavioral and demographic data from publishers for ad-targeting purposes. As a data aggregation and marketing intelligence tool, it raises standard third-party tracking and data-brokerage privacy concerns, but no specific fraud or phishing abuse has been documented.

Duda.history: Duda was founded in 2009 by Itai Sadan and Amir Glatt, initially launching as a mobile-focused website builder. The company pivoted in 2014 to focus on professional web design agencies and white-label deployments for hosting companies and digital agencies. Duda raised $50 million in Series D funding in June 2021, bringing total funding to $100 million. The company is headquartered in Palo Alto and focuses on serving digital agencies and SMB-focused platforms.
Duda.summary_abuse: Duda is a white-label website builder platform used by agencies and hosting companies. Like other site builders, Duda-powered sites could theoretically be used to create phishing or scam pages. Duda enforces terms of service and responds to abuse reports. No notable documented incidents of widespread abuse on the Duda platform have been reported.

Duel.history: Duel was founded in 2015 in London by Paul Archer and Panagiotis Tsarouchis. Archer, a world-record-breaking adventurer and former brand ambassador, co-founded Duel to help consumer brands build brand advocacy programs. The company raised £1.8 million in 2020 and £2.5 million in 2022 from investors including Downing Ventures and Publicis Groupe. Duel works with brands including Charlotte Tilbury, Monica Vinader, and ELEMIS.
Duel.summary_abuse: Duel is a B2B brand advocacy platform used by consumer brands to manage affiliate and influencer programs. The platform processes social media and influencer data for legitimate marketing purposes. No documented incidents of abuse have been reported.

Dukaan.history: Dukaan was founded in May-June 2020 by Suumit Shah, Subhash Choudhary, and Anurag Meena in Bangalore, India during the COVID-19 lockdown to help small merchants quickly create online stores via smartphones. The platform built 150,000 stores in its first 20 days. Dukaan is considered India's answer to Shopify, enabling merchants without technical skills to go online. The company raised funding from investors and expanded significantly in 2020-2021.
Dukaan.summary_abuse: Dukaan is an Indian e-commerce platform that allows merchants to create online stores. As a free-tier commerce platform with easy signup, it carries moderate abuse risk as fraudulent merchants could create fake storefronts. The platform's rapid growth in India means abuse monitoring is critical. No major documented incidents of widespread fraud through Dukaan have been specifically reported.

Dukany.history: Dukany is a subscription-based e-commerce platform that enables small businesses to create online stores with integrated payment processing and WhatsApp API integration for customer engagement. The platform targets small to medium businesses seeking a simple online store presence with social commerce capabilities.
Dukany.summary_abuse: Dukany is a legitimate e-commerce SaaS platform with no known abuse incidents. The platform is niche, targeting small businesses, with limited public security documentation and no known compliance certifications.

Duopana.history: Duopana was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in Curitiba, Parana, Brazil. It is a community management SaaS platform that allows creators and businesses to build online communities, sell memberships, online courses, and manage private groups. The platform provides forums, chat rooms, event calendars, file sharing, and member management tools with analytics.
Duopana.summary_abuse: Duopana is a small community platform startup with no known abuse incidents. It is a niche Brazilian startup with limited public security documentation, no compliance certifications found, and low enterprise adoption.

Durable.history: Durable was founded in 2022 by James Clift and Ross Howard-Jones and is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada. The company builds AI-powered website creation tools targeting small businesses, with a reported 3 million users. The platform uses generative AI to create websites in under 30 seconds and also includes CRM and invoicing tools for SMBs.
Durable.summary_abuse: Durable is a legitimate AI website builder startup with no known abuse incidents or security concerns. The company is VC-backed and growing rapidly. No compliance certifications were found publicly. The platform targets non-technical small business owners with simple website needs.

DutchIS.history: DutchIS is a Netherlands-based hosting and infrastructure provider located in Zwolle, Overijssel. The company designs and manufactures its own hardware and software, operates a fully redundant datacenter powered by green energy from wind turbines, and provides cloud hosting, VPS, and dedicated server services. It is known for its focus on sustainability and knowledgeable support team.
DutchIS.summary_abuse: DutchIS is a legitimate Dutch infrastructure and hosting provider with no known abuse incidents. The company emphasizes sustainability and green energy. It is a niche provider with limited public security certification information but is GDPR-compliant as a Netherlands-based company.

Dweet.history: Dweet.io is an IoT messaging platform created by Bug Labs that allows connected devices to publish and subscribe to data in real time via a simple HTTP API, described as Twitter for social machines. The platform requires no setup or sign-up for basic use. It powers IoT development environments for large organizations including Verizon Thingspace and Ford Motor Company's OpenXC platform.
Dweet.summary_abuse: Dweet.io is a legitimate IoT data messaging platform with no known abuse incidents. The free public tier can potentially be used to share sensitive device data inadvertently, as there is no authentication by default. The platform is primarily used by hobbyists and IoT developers for prototyping.

Dwolla.history: Dwolla was founded in 2008 by Ben Milne in Des Moines, Iowa, initially as a consumer payment service before pivoting to B2B ACH payment infrastructure. The company raised significant VC funding and serves approximately 650 clients with 30 million end users. Dwolla provides a payment API for businesses to facilitate bank transfers and ACH payments without handling card numbers.
Dwolla.summary_abuse: Dwolla is a legitimate B2B payment infrastructure provider with strong security practices including SOC2 Type 2 and PCI DSS compliance. No significant abuse incidents are known. The company is US-based, regulated as a money services business, and focused on enterprise ACH payment workflows.

DX.history: DX Group was founded in 1975 during UK postal strikes by Pauline Lyle-Smith and Henry Seymour as an alternative delivery network. It expanded into freight by acquiring Nightfreight GB (rebranded DX Freight) in 2012 and is publicly listed on AIM.
DX.summary_abuse: DX is a UK freight and mail carrier. Like other major parcel carriers, DX delivery notifications are occasionally spoofed in phishing campaigns, but this is impersonation rather than abuse of the DX platform itself. No significant documented platform-level abuse.

DX1.history: DX1 was launched in 2013-2014 by Dominion Powersports Solutions (a division of Dominion Enterprises) as an all-in-one cloud-based dealership management platform for the motorcycle and powersports industry, combining DMS, CRM, website and marketing tools.
DX1.summary_abuse: DX1 is a vertical SaaS platform for powersports dealerships with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B dealership management system with no consumer-facing content-hosting surface.

DynAd.history: DynAd Systems is a Brazilian ad server and programmatic media buying platform offering algorithm-driven campaign management, fraud detection, viewability tracking, and brand safety tools. The platform supports programmatic buying modalities including open auction, private auction, and programmatic guaranteed, with proprietary optimization algorithms targeting CPC, CPA, and ROI delivery.
DynAd.summary_abuse: DynAd is an advertising technology platform with no major public abuse incidents, though as an ad network it carries inherent tracker and privacy risk. The platform includes brand safety features and fraud detection. It is a niche Brazilian ad tech company with limited public security documentation.

Dynamic Conditions.history: Dynamic Conditions is an open-source WordPress plugin that adds conditional logic to Elementor widgets, requiring Elementor Pro for dynamic tag support. It allows showing or hiding elements based on dynamic tag comparisons including date conditions and user status checks. The plugin has over 70,000 active installations and is maintained by RTO Websites on GitHub.
Dynamic Conditions.summary_abuse: Dynamic Conditions is a legitimate open-source WordPress plugin with no known abuse incidents. It is GPL-licensed, actively maintained, and widely used for Elementor conditional logic. No security vulnerabilities or malicious behavior have been publicly reported.

Dynamic Yield.history: Dynamic Yield was founded in 2011 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Liad Agmon and Omri Mendellevich as a personalization and recommendation platform. McDonald's acquired it for approximately $327 million in 2019 to power drive-thru menu personalization. Mastercard then acquired it from McDonald's in 2022, integrating it into Mastercard's Data and Services division to offer personalization capabilities to retailers and financial services clients.
Dynamic Yield.summary_abuse: Dynamic Yield is a legitimate enterprise personalization platform with strong institutional ownership under Mastercard. No significant abuse incidents are known. The platform is an ad-tech adjacent tool that collects user behavioral data for personalization, carrying moderate privacy risk typical of recommendation engines. SOC2 Type 2 certified.

Dynamics.js.history: Dynamics.js is an open-source JavaScript library created by Michael Villar for creating physics-based CSS animations and animating SVG elements or JavaScript objects. The library supports spring, bounce, gravity, bezier, and ease-in-out animation types. It is available on npm and via CDN, and was first published around 2015.
Dynamics.js.summary_abuse: Dynamics.js is a safe MIT-licensed open-source animation library with no known abuse incidents. The project appears to be in a dormant state with no recent active maintenance. There are no known security vulnerabilities or malicious behavior.

Dynamicweb.history: DynamicWeb was founded in 1999 by Christian Beer and Nicolai Hoeg Pedersen in Aarhus, Denmark, celebrating 25 years of operation in 2024. It provides an all-in-one commerce suite combining e-commerce, PIM (Product Information Management), CMS, and digital marketing in a single platform. The company serves over 2,000 brands across manufacturing, distribution, retail and B2B verticals through a network of 300+ global partners.
Dynamicweb.summary_abuse: DynamicWeb is a long-established Danish e-commerce and CMS platform with no known abuse incidents. The company has operated for over 25 years and has strong mid-market enterprise adoption in Europe. GDPR-compliant by design as a Danish company. No security incidents or malicious behavior reported.

Dynatrace.history: Dynatrace was founded in 2005 by Bernd Greifeneder, Sok-Kheng Taing, and Hubert Gerstmayr in Linz, Austria as dynaTrace Software GmbH. The company pioneered application performance management (APM) and evolved into a comprehensive AI-powered observability platform. Dynatrace went public on the NYSE under ticker DT in 2019. It serves thousands of enterprise customers globally across cloud monitoring, infrastructure observability, security, and digital experience.
Dynatrace.summary_abuse: Dynatrace is a highly trusted publicly-listed enterprise observability and APM platform with no known abuse incidents. The company holds SOC2 Type 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS certifications. It is widely adopted in enterprise environments and has strong security practices including a bug bounty program.

Dynatrace RUM.history: Dynatrace RUM (Real User Monitoring) is a product module within the Dynatrace AI-powered observability platform, built by Dynatrace Inc. (founded 2005, Linz, Austria, NYSE:DT). The RUM module monitors actual end-user interactions with web and mobile applications in real time, providing session playback, performance waterfall analysis, error tracking, and conversion funnel analytics for enterprises.
Dynatrace RUM.summary_abuse: Dynatrace RUM is a product component of the publicly listed, SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR-certified Dynatrace platform. No abuse incidents are known. As a real user monitoring tool it collects session-level user behavior data, which has inherent privacy implications, but the platform provides GDPR-compliant data handling and consent management.

Dyte.history: Dyte was founded in 2020 in Bangalore, India by Abhishek Kankani, Kushagra Vaish, and Palash Golecha. The company provides a live video and audio SDK enabling developers to integrate real-time video calling into web, mobile, and desktop applications. In April 2022, Dyte raised $11.6 million in seed funding from Unbound, Sequoia Capital India's Surge, and Nexus Venture Partners, after graduating from Y Combinator.
Dyte.summary_abuse: Dyte is a legitimate video SDK startup backed by top-tier investors with no known abuse incidents. The company is SOC2 Type 2 certified and GDPR-compliant. No security incidents have been publicly reported. The product is a developer-focused video infrastructure tool used for legitimate communication applications.
